sources.full_name: IADI Core Principles for Effective Deposit Insurance Systems
sources.coverage_note: All 16 IADI Core Principles with assessment criteria.

provisions.title: CP 1 - Public Policy Objectives
provisions.text: The first step in adopting or reforming a deposit insurance system is to specify appropriate public policy objectives that the deposit insurance system is expected to achieve. These objectives should be formally specified and should be part of the design of the deposit insurance system. The principal objectives of a deposit insurance system are to: protect depositors (in particular, small and less financially sophisticated depositors); contribute to financial stability; and help mitigate the consequences of bank failures. The public policy objectives should be clearly defined in legislation.

provisions.title: CP 2 - Mandate and Powers
provisions.text: The deposit insurer's mandate and powers should be clearly defined and formally specified in legislation. The mandate should be consistent with the public policy objectives. The deposit insurer should have the necessary powers to fulfil its mandate, which may include: collecting premiums/levies; managing the deposit insurance fund; reimbursing depositors; participating in resolution proceedings; and pursuing recoveries from failed institutions.

provisions.title: CP 3 - Governance
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should be operationally independent, transparent, accountable, and insulated from undue political and industry influence. The governance structure should include: a governing body with clear responsibilities; appropriate checks and balances; transparent decision-making processes; accountability to the legislature or government; and public disclosure of the deposit insurer's activities and financial position.

provisions.title: CP 4 - Relationships with Other Safety-Net Participants
provisions.text: There should be a formal and comprehensive framework in place for the close coordination and information sharing, on a routine basis as well as in relation to particular banks, among the deposit insurer and other financial system safety-net participants. Safety-net participants include the central bank, prudential regulator/supervisor, resolution authority, ministry of finance, and any other relevant bodies. Information-sharing agreements should be in place and should cover both normal times and crisis situations.

provisions.title: CP 5 - Cross-Border Issues
provisions.text: Where there are material cross-border considerations, the deposit insurer should engage in cross-border coordination and information sharing through formal agreements. This includes: home-host coordination for branches and subsidiaries; information sharing on the deposit insurance arrangements applicable to depositors; coordination of reimbursement in the event of a cross-border bank failure; and participation in crisis management groups for cross-border banks.

provisions.title: CP 6 - Deposit Insurer's Role in Contingency Planning and Crisis Management
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should be part of the framework within the financial system safety net for contingency planning and crisis management. The deposit insurer should have access to information necessary to prepare for and carry out its responsibilities in contingency planning and crisis management, including information on the condition of member institutions and the financial system as a whole.

provisions.title: CP 7 - Membership
provisions.text: Membership in the deposit insurance system should be compulsory for all financial institutions accepting deposits from those deemed most in need of protection (for example, retail and small business depositors). The criteria for membership should be clearly defined in legislation. There should be no institution that accepts covered deposits outside the deposit insurance system.

provisions.title: CP 8 - Coverage
provisions.text: Coverage should be limited, credible and cover the large majority of depositors but leave a substantial amount of deposits exposed to market discipline. The coverage level should be set to protect the majority of depositors in full, but should leave a sufficient proportion of deposits and depositors unprotected to maintain incentives for market discipline. The coverage limit should be clearly defined and communicated to the public. In practice, the coverage level should fully protect approximately 90-95% of depositors.

provisions.title: CP 9 - Sources and Uses of Funds
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should have readily available funds and all funding mechanisms necessary to ensure prompt reimbursement of depositors' claims, including a pre-funded deposit insurance fund. The fund should be built up through regular premiums or levies on member institutions. The target fund size should be established based on an assessment of the deposit insurer's potential exposure. Back-up funding arrangements (such as a government line of credit) should be in place for extraordinary circumstances.

provisions.title: CP 10 - Public Awareness
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should promote public awareness of the deposit insurance system. The public should be informed about the benefits and limitations of the deposit insurance system, including which institutions are members, what types of deposits are covered, the coverage limits, and the reimbursement process. Awareness programmes should be conducted on an ongoing basis and should target all relevant stakeholders, including depositors, member institutions, and the media.

provisions.title: CP 11 - Legal Protection
provisions.text: The deposit insurer and individuals working for the deposit insurer should be protected against lawsuits for their decisions and actions taken in good faith while discharging their mandates. Legal protection should cover the deposit insurer itself, its governing body members, and its employees. Legal costs arising from actions taken in good faith should be borne by the deposit insurer or the government.

provisions.title: CP 12 - Dealing with Parties at Fault
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should be part of, or party to, a framework within the financial system safety net that provides for the ability to seek legal redress against those parties at fault in a bank failure. This includes the ability to pursue claims against directors, officers, auditors, and others whose actions or omissions contributed to the bank's failure. The deposit insurer should have the power to pursue recoveries from those at fault.

provisions.title: CP 13 - Early Detection and Timely Intervention
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should be part of a framework within the financial system safety net that provides for the early detection and timely intervention and resolution of troubled member institutions. Early intervention mechanisms should include: early warning systems; prompt corrective action frameworks; powers to impose supervisory actions on weakening institutions; and procedures for coordination among safety-net participants.

provisions.title: CP 14 - Failure Resolution
provisions.text: An effective resolution regime should: allow for the timely resolution of banks; provide for a range of resolution tools including deposit transfer, purchase and assumption, and bridge bank arrangements; protect covered depositors; minimise the cost of resolution; and impose losses on shareholders and unsecured creditors in a manner consistent with the creditor hierarchy. Where the deposit insurer has a role in resolution, it should have the necessary powers, tools, and processes.

provisions.title: CP 15 - Reimbursing Depositors
provisions.text: The deposit insurance system should provide for the prompt reimbursement of depositors' covered deposits. The target should be to reimburse depositors within seven working days. To achieve prompt reimbursement, the deposit insurer should have: access to depositor information through a single customer view (SCV); pre-established processes and systems for reimbursement; and agreements with member institutions to ensure timely provision of depositor data.

provisions.title: CP 16 - Recoveries
provisions.text: The deposit insurer should have, by law, the right to recover its claims from the estate of failed member institutions. The deposit insurer should be subrogated to the rights of depositors it has reimbursed. The deposit insurer should rank at least pari passu with depositors in the liquidation of a failed member institution. The deposit insurer should have adequate processes and resources to pursue and maximise recoveries in a timely manner.

definitions.definition: A system established to protect depositors against the loss of their insured deposits in the event that a bank is unable to meet its obligations to depositors. The system typically involves the collection of premiums from member institutions and the maintenance of a fund to reimburse depositors.

A consolidated record of all eligible deposit accounts held by a single depositor with a given bank, enabling the deposit insurer to determine quickly the total insured amount due to each depositor in the event of a bank failure.
